raw_text:
Termination for default

Termination for default applies when the contractor fails to fulfill their obligations under the contract, usually through non-performance or delayed delivery. Not acting promptly after a contractor is in default can negatively affect Canada's interests.

The decision to terminate a contract for default should be made only after all other possible solutions have been explored. You must consult with Legal Services early to ensure that the proposed actions will not prejudice Canada's legal position and that the termination is legally enforceable.

If a contract is secured by surety bonds, termination may change the contractual relationship with the bonding company.

Reasons to terminate for default

Canada has the right to terminate all or any part of the contract for default for the reasons described below.

The contractor fails to make progress: If the contractor's lack of progress endangers contract performance, the buyer may provide the contractor written notice to rectify the situation within a period of ten days. If this period must be longer, you may require the contractor to show evidence of corrective action within ten days. If a contractor does not rectify the situation within ten days, you may, subject to the limitation in the default clause, initiate action to terminate the contract for default.

The contractor fails to perform any provision of the contract: If the contractor does not rectify such a defect within ten days of receipt of a notice from the buyer, the buyer may, within the limitations in the default clause, initiate action to terminate the contract in whole or in part for default.

The contractor fails to deliver the goods or perform the services within the time specified in the contract: In the absence of excusable delays, Canada has the right to terminate a contract immediately, for any delay, no matter how slight. This includes the right to accept or reject goods shipped, but not yet delivered.

Canada can also terminate for default if the contractor does make timely delivery but delivers defective goods, improperly performs services, and is unable to take corrective action within the unexpired delivery schedule period.

If you are contemplating termination of a contract for failure to deliver on time, you must advise the contractor of this promptly after the default occurs. Failure to take such action may prejudice Canada’s position.

When there is reasonable assurance that delivery will be made, even though late, it may be desirable to discuss extension of the delivery time with client. If the delivery date is extended, negotiation for some kind of consideration may be appropriate. This constitutes a conditional amendment which triggers the requirement for a vendor performance corrective measure (VPCM) assessment.

The contractor becomes bankrupt or insolvent: Upon receipt of a notice of a contractor’s bankruptcy or insolvency, you should follow the settlement procedure for final payments.

Action to recover loss or damage

After a contract is terminated you will determine the actual amount or best estimate of loss or damage suffered by Canada. You will also determine the distribution of the damages to be recovered from the contractor. Estimates include any amount exceeding the original contract price which Canada may have to incur in procuring the goods or services elsewhere.

You must refer claims to Legal Services when a contract is secured by a security deposit (government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, and irrevocable standby letters of credit). You must also refer claims to Legal Services when Canada and the contractor have claims against each other related to the same work package.

In all other cases, you will try to negotiate a settlement. If a satisfactory settlement cannot be reached, you will refer the claim to Legal Services for action.

Settlement agreement

When a contractor agrees with the proposed settlement, the recommendation to recover monies will be submitted to the Cost and Profit Assurance Group (CPAG), or, in the case of a Canadian Commercial Corporation (CCC) contract, the director, Finance and Resources Administration (FRA). The Cost Audit Group or the director, FRA , will issue an invoice to the contractor for the monetary recovery.

Follow-up on payment

If payment is not received within 60 days from the date of issue of the invoice, then CPAG or the director, FRA , will advise you to take follow-up action with the contractor. If normal follow-up procedures are unsuccessful, the matter must be referred to Legal Services.

Record keeping

Claims must remain in departmental records until fully resolved through payment or a properly authorized deletion action.

Contract payment under surety bond

When a surety bond is being enforced, payments will be issued as follows:

Performance bond: After the contract is completed to Canada's satisfaction, the bonding company may be paid all amounts the contractor would have been entitled to under the terms of the contract.

Payment bond: The bonding company must not be reimbursed for the payment of creditors from any funds held by Canada until the work is complete, and the surety company has fully discharged its obligations under the bond.
markdown_text:
Termination for default

Termination for default applies when the contractor fails to fulfill their obligations under the contract, usually through non-performance or delayed delivery. Not acting promptly after a contractor is in default can negatively affect Canada's interests.

The decision to terminate a contract for default should be made only after all other possible solutions have been explored. You must consult with Legal Services early to ensure that the proposed actions will not prejudice Canada's legal position and that the termination is legally enforceable.

If a contract is secured by surety bonds, termination may change the contractual relationship with the bonding company.

Reasons to terminate for default

Canada has the right to terminate all or any part of the contract for default for the reasons described below.

The contractor fails to make progress: If the contractor's lack of progress endangers contract performance, the buyer may provide the contractor written notice to rectify the situation within a period of ten days. If this period must be longer, you may require the contractor to show evidence of corrective action within ten days. If a contractor does not rectify the situation within ten days, you may, subject to the limitation in the default clause, initiate action to terminate the contract for default.

The contractor fails to perform any provision of the contract: If the contractor does not rectify such a defect within ten days of receipt of a notice from the buyer, the buyer may, within the limitations in the default clause, initiate action to terminate the contract in whole or in part for default.

The contractor fails to deliver the goods or perform the services within the time specified in the contract: In the absence of excusable delays, Canada has the right to terminate a contract immediately, for any delay, no matter how slight. This includes the right to accept or reject goods shipped, but not yet delivered.

Canada can also terminate for default if the contractor does make timely delivery but delivers defective goods, improperly performs services, and is unable to take corrective action within the unexpired delivery schedule period.

If you are contemplating termination of a contract for failure to deliver on time, you must advise the contractor of this promptly after the default occurs. Failure to take such action may prejudice Canada’s position.

When there is reasonable assurance that delivery will be made, even though late, it may be desirable to discuss extension of the delivery time with client. If the delivery date is extended, negotiation for some kind of consideration may be appropriate. This constitutes a conditional amendment which triggers the requirement for a vendor performance corrective measure (VPCM) assessment.

The contractor becomes bankrupt or insolvent: Upon receipt of a notice of a contractor’s bankruptcy or insolvency, you should follow the settlement procedure for final payments.

Action to recover loss or damage

After a contract is terminated you will determine the actual amount or best estimate of loss or damage suffered by Canada. You will also determine the distribution of the damages to be recovered from the contractor. Estimates include any amount exceeding the original contract price which Canada may have to incur in procuring the goods or services elsewhere.

You must refer claims to Legal Services when a contract is secured by a security deposit (government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, and irrevocable standby letters of credit). You must also refer claims to Legal Services when Canada and the contractor have claims against each other related to the same work package.

In all other cases, you will try to negotiate a settlement. If a satisfactory settlement cannot be reached, you will refer the claim to Legal Services for action.

Settlement agreement

When a contractor agrees with the proposed settlement, the recommendation to recover monies will be submitted to the Cost and Profit Assurance Group (CPAG), or, in the case of a Canadian Commercial Corporation (CCC) contract, the director, Finance and Resources Administration (FRA). The Cost Audit Group or the director, FRA , will issue an invoice to the contractor for the monetary recovery.

Follow-up on payment

If payment is not received within 60 days from the date of issue of the invoice, then CPAG or the director, FRA , will advise you to take follow-up action with the contractor. If normal follow-up procedures are unsuccessful, the matter must be referred to Legal Services.

Record keeping

Claims must remain in departmental records until fully resolved through payment or a properly authorized deletion action.

Contract payment under surety bond

When a surety bond is being enforced, payments will be issued as follows:

Performance bond: After the contract is completed to Canada's satisfaction, the bonding company may be paid all amounts the contractor would have been entitled to under the terms of the contract.

Payment bond: The bonding company must not be reimbursed for the payment of creditors from any funds held by Canada until the work is complete, and the surety company has fully discharged its obligations under the bond.
